coredata.dc:title: Visible and fluorescent detection of melamine in raw milk with one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Na
Liu, Ting
Liu, Shi Gang
Lin, Shu Min
Fan, Yu Zhu
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: Using carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer, a visible and fluorescent method was developed for melamine detection in raw milk with one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles. In this work, carbon dots (C-dots) were applied to reduce silver ions and stabilize the nanoparticles, resulting in the formation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). As a result, the inherent fluorescence emission of C-dots was significantly reduced after the formation of AgNPs. However, in the presence of melamine, silver ions could interact with the nitrogen atoms in amine and triazine groups of melamine. With 0–2μM melamine, aggregated AgNPs were found after the reduction by C-dots, resulting in color and absorbance changes. With further increase of melamine (2–20μM), both formation and aggregation of AgNPs were inhibited, and the fluorescence was gradually increased. This optical platform was optimized for melamine detection and then was applied to detecting melamine in raw milk samples. The results for melamine assay based on visible and fluorescent method showed the requisite sensitivity with a low detection limit of 30nM, as well as high selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible and fluorescent method
Melamine
Carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence detection of melamine
Analytical performance of the melamine sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of C-dots and the inset is the size distribution histogram. (B) FTIR spectrum of C-dots. (C) TEM image of one-step synthesized AgNPs using C-dots as the reducing and stabilizing reagent. (D) HRTEM image of the AgNPs.
Melamine determination with one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of different samples (curves 1–6). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of different samples (curves 1–6). (C) TEM image of AgNPs synthesized with the addition of melamine (2μM). (D) TEM image of AgNPs synthesized with the addition of melamine (14μM).

(A) Color changes of solutions corresponding to different amounts of melamine (a–q: 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 1.0, 1.4, 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20μM). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra with different concentrations of melamine. Inset: linear relationship between the absorbance ratio and the concentration of melamine. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra with different concentrations of melamine. Inset: linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of melamine. Concentrations: AgNO3 (0.06mM), C-dots (0.12mgmL−1), and NaOH (2.8mM).

Specificity evaluation of the optical platform for melamine determination. The concentrations of melamine and the amino acids were 4μM. Concentrations: AgNO3 (0.06mM), C-dots (0.12mgmL−1), NaOH (2.8mM), urea (10mM), glucose (1mM), and lactose (155mM).

UV–vis absorption spectra of the sample: without any addition (curve 1); with the extract from blank raw milk added (curve 2); with final concentration of 0.2μM (0.025ppm, curve 3); with final concentration of 1μM (0.125ppm, curve 4); with final concentration: 2μM (0.25ppm, curve 5). Inset: the corresponding visual color change. Concentrations: AgNO3 (0.06mM), C-dots (0.12mgmL−1), and NaOH (2.8mM).

head.ce:title: Visible and fluorescent detection of melamine in raw milk with one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using carbon dots as the reductant and stabilizer, a visible and fluorescent method was developed for melamine detection in raw milk with one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles. In this work, carbon dots (C-dots) were applied to reduce silver ions and stabilize the nanoparticles, resulting in the formation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). As a result, the inherent fluorescence emission of C-dots was significantly reduced after the formation of AgNPs. However, in the presence of melamine, silver ions could interact with the nitrogen atoms in amine and triazine groups of melamine. With 0–2μM melamine, aggregated AgNPs were found after the reduction by C-dots, resulting in color and absorbance changes. With further increase of melamine (2–20μM), both formation and aggregation of AgNPs were inhibited, and the fluorescence was gradually increased. This optical platform was optimized for melamine detection and then was applied to detecting melamine in raw milk samples. The results for melamine assay based on visible and fluorescent method showed the requisite sensitivity with a low detection limit of 30nM, as well as high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Melamine (1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triamine) is an organic compound that serves as raw material for the production of melamine resins. As melamine contains a high level of nitrogen (66% by mass), the illegal addition of melamine to dairy products can lead to significant enhancement of protein content that is measured by using the Kjeldahl method. The continuous ingestion of melamine over the safety limit (2.5ppm in USA and EU; 1ppm for infant formula in China) could result in the formation of kidney stones, kidney failure, and even death in infants [1–4]. As recorded in 2008 Chinese milk scandal [5], melamine-tainted infant formula caused an estimated 300,000 victims in total, which involved the death of several children. Melamine concentrations in the adulterated milk products were as high as ∼3300ppm, posing an extreme danger to consumers [6]. Thus, it is becoming increasingly important to obtain highly selective and sensitive determination of melamine in dairy products [7]. Recent works on visual detection of melamine have provided an attractive route to achieving simple methods that afford requisite sensitivity and high selectivity [8–11].

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been widely used as their optical properties can be tuned by controlling particle size and shape [12–14]. When AgNPs get together in close proximity, their surface plasmons interact to shift resonant excitation, resulting in color variation from yellow to brown (or pale red). AgNPs have been utilized to visually detect melamine and these strategies generally follow the steps: synthesis of AgNPs, further modification of AgNPs, and melamine determination [10,15,16]. Achieving modified AgNPs usually requires toxic reagents, complex procedures, and time-consuming process, leading to the limited applications of these sensors. Thus, establishing a more facile and rapid assay is still in need for one-step melamine sensing in raw milk samples.

Recently, tremendous attention has been paid to the water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) owing to their tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, and biocompatibility, which render C-dots great potential for sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and photocatalysis [17–19]. The functional groups on their surface can be tailored based on the source and procedure during the synthesis process. C-dots can act as both the reducing and stabilizing reagent by virtue of the abundant hydroxyl moieties on their surface [20–23]. In 2009, Wang and co-workers reported that the silver ions could be reduced to elemental silver by irradiating an aqueous solution of C-dots and AgNO3 [24]. Additionally, employing C-dots as the reducing and stabilizing reagent has been recently reported for the synthesis of AgNPs [20].

In this assay, one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles with C-dots as the reducing and stabilizing reagent were employed for visible and fluorescent detection of melamine in raw milk. C-dots played an important role in reducing silver ions and stabilizing the nanoparticles. When C-dots were added to the silver ions in alkaline solution, monodispersed AgNPs would be obtained, resulting in remarkably reduced fluorescence emission. Aggregated AgNPs were observed when melamine was present. Visual color changes corresponding to the UV–vis absorption spectra were observed. Moreover, the fluorescence signals would be increased with the further increase of melamine. As a result, a dual optical platform combined UV–vis absorption and fluorescence signals could be achieved through the addition of melamine into the system.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phenylalanine (Phe), glutamine (Glu), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), cysteine (Cys), melamine, and ethylene glycol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Glucose and lactose were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co., Ltd., Buchs, German. The raw milk was obtained from the local supermarket. Trichloroacetic acid, acetonitrile, and other reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (specific resistance of 18.2MΩcm) was used throughout the whole experiment.

UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence emission spectra in the 380–600nm range were determined by monitoring fluorescence emission under excitation at 360nm. The morphologies of nanoparticles were collected on a JEOL-2100 system operated at 200kV (Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Germany).

Carbon dots (C-dots) were hydrothermally prepared according to a previous report with some modifications [20]. Briefly, 10mL of ethylene glycol and 600μL of sodium hydroxide solution (1M) were mixed together, and the mixture was thereafter heated at 180°C for 2h in a 25mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained pale yellow C-dots solution was collected and dialyzed against water for two days to remove excess reagents through a dialysis film (Mw 1000). The resulting nanoparticles were lyophilized and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence detection of melamine
ce:section.ce:para: In the melamine sensing assay, 30μL of AgNO3 (1mM), 50μL of melamine with different concentrations, and 370μL of water were added together. After that, 25μL of C-dots (2.4mgmL−1) and 25μL of NaOH (56mM) were added and the mixture was incubated at 37°C for 30min. Finally, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was measured at the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths (λex =360nm, λem =450nm).

The raw milk samples were prepared according to a reported work with few modifications [25]. Briefly, 15mL of 61mM trichloroacetic acid and 5mL of acetonitrile were added to 2g of raw milk to remove the proteins. Then, these samples were sonicated for 10min and stirred for 10min. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 10min, and the supernatant was adjusted to pH 6.8 and filtered through a 0.22mm nylon filter for three times to remove lipids. For recovery experiments, the raw milk samples were spiked with different concentrations of melamine before the extraction procedure, and these samples were treated with the same extraction experiment as the blank raw milk sample.

Optical characterization of C-dots produced from ethylene glycol is shown in Fig. S1. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits strong peak absorption at 261nm and a shoulder at about 320nm because of the π–π* transition of CC bonds and n–π* transition of CO bonds, respectively [26–28]. When the C-dots are excited at 360nm, their fluorescence emission spectrum shows a peak centered at 450nm (Fig. S1A). Additionally, a significant peak shift of the emission spectrum is discovered upon increasing excitation wavelength from 330 to 400nm (Fig. S1B). The morphology of C-dots was characterized using TEM. These C-dots are well dispersed and have an average size around 3nm as shown in the TEM image in Fig. 1 A. The functional groups on C-dots were evaluated by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B). The characteristic peaks at 3414 and 1415cm−1 could be assigned to OH bonds [29,30]. The rich hydroxyl groups on C-dots are thought to be originated from hydroxyl groups of ethylene glycol. The peaks centered at 1595 and 1082cm−1 signify the CO and CO bonds, respectively [31]. These functional groups provide the ability for the C-dots to act as both the reducing and stabilizing reagent.

By virtue of the aforementioned functions of C-dots, AgNPs were successfully synthesized in alkaline solution. The TEM image of AgNPs shows spherical nanoparticles with the diameter around 15nm as shown in Fig. 1C. Furthermore, the HRTEM image of AgNPs (Fig. 1D) displays a 0.24nm lattice spacing, which corresponds to the (111) metallic Ag lattice space [32]. Additionally, significant changes in both the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots were seen (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2A, the strong peak absorption appears at around 415nm, which agrees with the typical surface plasmon resonance absorption of dispersed AgNPs. The decreased fluorescence of C-dots also demonstrates the reduction of silver ions with C-dots (Fig. S2B). Ding et al. reported that C-dots could emit strong fluorescence mainly because of the radiative recombination of their surface-confined electrons and holes [33]. With C-dots participating in the reduction of silver ions, the nucleation began at the surface of C-dots that hold the electrons, and thus the radiative electron–hole recombination on their surface was inhibited, resulting in reduced fluorescence emission [21,34,35].

Recent reports have described that hydroxyl-rich C-dots can reduce silver ions to AgNPs without any other reducing reagents [20,36]. In this work, C-dots functioned as both the reductant and stabilizer for the preparation of AgNPs as well as a fluorescent reporter for sensing melamine (Fig. 2 ). After C-dots were added to AgNO3 in alkaline solution, the solution turned bright yellow, which indicated the formation of monodispersed AgNPs [37,38]. Furthermore, the fluorescence of C-dots was significantly decreased after the formation of AgNPs. When 0–2μM melamine was present with AgNO3 in alkaline solution, aggregated AgNPs were obtained after reduction and resulted in a remarkable change in the peak absorption. Additionally, both the formation and aggregation of AgNPs would be inhibited with further increase of melamine (2–20μM), leading to increased fluorescence intensity. Based on the variation of the peak absorption and fluorescence intensity, the dual optical determination of melamine was developed with one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as the reducing and stabilizing reagent.

In this assay, optical characterization combining the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra was carried out to evaluate the feasibility of this work. As shown in Fig. 3 , C-dots and sodium hydroxide serve important functions for the reduction of silver ions to AgNPs. No reaction takes place in the absence of C-dots or sodium hydroxide (curves 1–4, Fig. 3A). The absorption peak at 415nm is observed in the presence of C-dots in alkaline solution (curve 5, Fig. 3A), which is the typical absorption of roughly spherical AgNPs because of the free electrons on their surface [39–41]. Besides, silver ions could interact with the nitrogen atoms in amine and triazine groups of melamine, which was characterized by FTIR spectra in Fig. S3. By adding melamine (0–2μM) to silver ions, melamine and silver ions interact with each other and the Ag+-melamine network structure is formed. Considering that the amount of melamine is small, the steric hindrance of melamine is not strong enough to prevent the access of C-dots to Ag+. Thus, aggregated AgNPs can be formed after the reduction by C-dots and the small absorption peak at 550nm is observed (curve 6, Fig. 3A), consistent with the TEM image in Fig. 3C. However, with further increase of melamine (2–20μM), the absorbance of 400–600nm is gradually decreased (curves 7 and 8, Fig. 3A). The steric hindrance induced by large amounts of melamine might gradually become strong enough to prevent the formation and aggregation of AgNPs, which is consistent with the TEM image in Fig. 3D.

The fluorescence response of C-dots in the melamine assay is displayed in Fig. 3B. By individually adding melamine, silver ions, or sodium hydroxide to C-dots solution, no significant change of the fluorescence emission was observed (curves 1–4, Fig. 3B). Furthermore, it has been proved that AgNPs could be synthesized after C-dots were added to silver ions in alkaline solution. During the reduction process, nucleation takes place at the surface sites of C-dots that host the electrons, and thus the corresponding fluorescence intensity decreases significantly with the formation of AgNPs (curve 5, Fig. 3B). It is concluded that C-dots are consumed as the reductant for the formation of both dispersed and aggregated AgNPs, giving rise to decreased fluorescence intensity. With 0–2μM melamine, aggregated AgNPs can be formed after the reduction by C-dots. The fluorescence intensity of the sample with melamine (0–2μM) is not obviously enhanced compared with that without melamine. However, when the melamine concentration increases from 2 to 20μM, the reduction process will be significantly inhibited by the steric hindrance of melamine. Under this circumstance, lesser C-dots are consumed as the reductant and show high fluorescence (curve 6, Fig. 3B). The presence of melamine gives rise to concentration-dependent UV–vis changes and fluorescence enhancement of the C-dots, which provides an attractive route to melamine detection by both UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy.

For sensitive melamine detection, relevant parameters including the concentrations of sodium hydroxide and C-dots and reaction time of the proposed method were optimized. Along with the increase of melamine, the absorbance at 550nm is increased, while the absorbance at 415nm is gradually decreased. Thus, the absorbance ratio (A 550/A 415) is employed for melamine determination. The ratio of fluorescence enhancement is defined as (F − F 0)/F 0, where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensities obtained in the presence and absence of melamine, respectively. To begin the optimization of the assay, pH values that tuned the reaction process were determined by controlling the concentration of NaOH. When the concentration of NaOH is 2.8mM (pH 10.5), the absorbance ratio (A 550/A 415) is the minimum without melamine but reaches the maximum with melamine. Thus, a high difference of the absorbance ratios (ΔA 550/A 415) with the presence and absence of melamine occurs with 2.8mM NaOH (Fig. S4A). Additionally, the ratio of fluorescence enhancement also shows the best performance with 2.8mM NaOH (Fig. S4B). Therefore, 2.8mM NaOH (pH 10.5) was chosen as the optimal concentration in the subsequent experiment.

The concentration of C-dots that acted as the reducing and stabilizing reagent was also determined (Fig. S5). By increasing the concentration of C-dots, the absorbance ratio (A 550/A 415) with melamine is gradually reduced. Furthermore, the absorbance ratio without melamine is decreased at first and then increased, resulting in the minimal background signal with 0.12mgmL−1 C-dots (Fig. S5A). The high difference of the absorbance ratios is found with the concentration of 0.12mgmL−1 C-dots, which is also confirmed by the signal of the fluorescence enhancement ratio (Fig. S5B). As a result, 0.12mgmL−1 C-dots made the best UV–vis absorption and fluorescence signals for this assay.

The effect of reaction time on the system was also studied and optimized (Fig. S6). Gradually increased absorbance ratios are obtained as the incubation time prolonged. Furthermore, both signals keep stable after a reaction time of 30min. Thus, 30min produced the optimal performance and was used in this assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the melamine sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of this work was evaluated by changing the concentration of melamine. Under optimum conditions, the color, absorbance, and fluorescence intensity were monitored with different concentrations of melamine. The color change of the synthesized AgNPs with melamine can be readily observed with the naked eye (Fig. 4 A). By asking a group of 20 individuals to read the results, the presence of as low as 0.6μM melamine can be easily identified with the naked eye according to the distinct color difference between the sample (photograph e) and the blank test (photograph a). The detection limit is quantified to be 0.6μM at a 95% confidence level using manual reading. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the system upon the addition of melamine are shown in Figs. 4B and S7. With increased melamine concentration in the 0–2μM range, the absorbance at 550nm is increased, whereas the absorbance at 415nm is gradually decreased (Fig. 4B). The absorbance ratio (A 550/A 415) shows a linear dependence on the melamine concentration. The detection limit of 30nM is calculated based on 3σ/slope (where σ was the standard deviation of the background signals). However, when the melamine concentration increases from 2 to 20μM, the absorbance of 400–600nm is gradually decreased (Fig. S7). As shown in the inset of Fig. S7, the absorbance at 445nm (A 445) exhibits a linear dependence on the melamine concentration.

The fluorescence emission intensity was also found sensitive to melamine, and the increased melamine resulted in significant fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 4C). The inset of Fig. 4C exhibits the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and melamine concentration from 0 to 14μM. For this fluorescence assay, the limit of detection is also estimated, and it is obtained as low as 600nM (based on 3σ/slope). The detection limits for both the absorption and fluorescence assays are comparable with those of other melamine assays (Table S1). Thus, the melamine detection method has the needed sensitivity and shows great potential for the application to low-resource settings.

To validate the selectivity of the detection method, we measured several different substances that might exist in milk, such as urea, glucose, lactose, and amino acids. It is found that the solution containing the potential interferents exhibit bright yellow color without remarkable formation of aggregated AgNPs (Fig. 5 , inset). As shown in Fig. 5, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence signals are measured, and only melamine leads to significant enhancement of both the absorbance ratio and fluorescence intensity, confirming the good selectivity for melamine. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S8, the difference of absorbance ratio and relative fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) recorded with melamine and other competing substances demonstrate that most of the coexistent substances had a very weak or negligible interfering effect on melamine detection. Further exploration of cysteine has been shown in Fig. S6 as its functional groups have been reported interacting with silver ions [40]. Upon the addition of cysteine, the formation of AgNPs was gradually seen with time despite its inhibition effect. High selectivity of the method can be achieved by controlling the reaction time.

To investigate whether the method could be applied for melamine detection, we evaluated the melamine determination in raw milk samples. As no melamine was found in the blank milk sample, recovery experiments spiked with different concentrations of melamine were carried out. As can be seen in Fig. 6 , visual color changes are achieved, consistent with the peak absorbance of UV–vis spectra. And the corresponding recoveries were calculated to be 95.71±3.8%, 113.58±4.3%, and 99.22±2.9% for melamine with the final concentration of 0.2, 1, and 2μM, respectively. The proposed method showed promise for the realization of dual-mode protocol for assaying melamine within a common platform.

In this work, one-step synthesized silver nanoparticles are employed to develop a visual and fluorescent melamine detection method. The visual assays are achieved through melamine-induced color changes during the formation of aggregated AgNPs, which was confirmed by the UV–vis absorption spectra. The fluorescence detection of melamine is developed based on the fluorescence enhancement of C-dots with increasing melamine. The method displays a low detection limit of 600nM (75.6ppb) by the naked eye, which is below the safety limit in USA and EU (2.5ppm) and China (1ppm). Moreover, the detection of melamine can also be performed by the UV–vis spectrophotometry and fluorescence method, which are also attractive and promising. The detection limit is obtained as low as 30nM (3.78ppb) using the UV–vis spectrophotometry. The good sensitivity and selectivity of this method, accompanied by the simple operations, make it an intriguing method for real-time and on-site melamine detection in low-resource areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174, 21675131), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYB16053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.03.068.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen-bonding recognition-induced color change of gold nanoparticles for visual detection of melamine in raw milk and infant formula
Portable detection of melamine in milk using a personal glucose meter based on an in vitro selected structure-switching aptamer
One-pot synthesis of mesoporous structured ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor for highly sensitive detection of melamine from milk samples
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Rapid detection of melamine in untreated milk and wheat gluten by ultrasound-assisted extractive electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (EESI-MS)
Detection of melamine in milk products by surface desorption atmospheric pressure chemical ionization mass spectrometry
An upconversion fluorescence resonance energy transfer nanosensor for one step detection of melamine in raw milk
One-step synthesis of silver/dopamine nanoparticles and visual detection of melamine in raw milk
Visual detection of melamine based on the peroxidase-like activity enhancement of bare gold nanoparticles
Sensing colorimetric approaches based on gold and silver nanoparticles aggregation: chemical creativity behind the assay. A review
A ratiometric nanosensor based on conjugated polyelectrolyte-stabilized AgNPs for ultrasensitive fluorescent and colorimetric sensing of melamine
Graphitic carbon nitride nanodots: as reductant for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles and its biothiols biosensing application
Localized surface plasmon resonance spectroscopy of single silver triangular nanoprisms
Immobilization of highly dispersed Ag nanoparticles on carbon nanotubes using electron-assisted reduction for antibacterial performance
Visual detection of melamine in infant formula at 0.1ppm level based on silver nanoparticles
Visual detection of melamine in raw milk by label-free silver nanoparticles
A novel and facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots via salep hydrothermal treatment as the silver nanoparticles support: application to electroanalytical determination of H2O2 in fetal bovine serum
Recent advances in carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and biomedical applications
Photoluminescent carbon nanodots: synthesis, physicochemical properties and analytical applications
Hydroxyl-rich C-dots synthesized by a one-pot method and their application in the preparation of noble metal nanoparticles
Carbon nanodots as ligand exchange probes in Au@C-dot nanobeacons for fluorescent turn-on detection of biothiols
Assay of biothiols by regulating the growth of silver nanoparticles with C-dots as reducing agent
Reduced state carbon dots as both reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of gold nanoparticles
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Rapid and highly-sensitive melamine sensing based on the efficient inner filter effect of Ag nanoparticles on the fluorescence of eco-friendly ZnSe quantum dots
One-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Novel fluorescent matrix embedded carbon quantum dots for the production of stable gold and silver hydrosols
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Graphene oxide: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application to glucose detection
Three-dimensional ordered mesoporous MnO2-supported Ag nanoparticles for catalytic removal of formaldehyde
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Carbon nanoparticles as visible-light photocatalysts for efficient CO2 conversion and beyond
Fenton-like reaction-mediated etching of gold nanorods for visual detection of Co2+
Silver nanoparticle anchored carbon dots for improved sensing, catalytic and intriguing antimicrobial activity
New tools for characterizing metallic nanoparticles: AgNPs a case study
Superoxide-mediated formation and charging of silver nanoparticles
Photochemical formation of silver nanoparticles in poly(N-vinylpyrrolidone)
Shape effects in plasmon resonance of individual colloidal silver nanoparticles
A highly selective and sensitive on-off sensor for silver ions and cysteine by light scattering technique of DNA-functionalized gold nanoparticles

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Na Li is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Ting Liu is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shi Gang Liu is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Shu Min Lin is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Yu Zhu Fan is a MS candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.

Hong Qun Luo is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.

Nian Bing Li is a professor of chemistry in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are the developments of electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.
